attributes.stem: A 59-year-old man is admitted to hospital for chemotherapy of a locally advanced carcinoma of the stomach. Two weeks ago, he recovered from an episode of cellulitis of his left leg which rendered him bed-bound for three days, but he has now returned to his functional baseline which is independently mobile. Three years ago, he had a myocardial infarction from which he made a good recovery. His BMI is 29. During his admission, he is considered for some form of venous thromboembolism (VTE) prophylaxis. It is anticipated he will be in hospital for approximately one week and go home with an intravenous infusion of cytotoxic therapy.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following would be the most appropriate form of VTE prophylaxis?
options: Graded compression stockings for the duration of the admission
Enoxaparin for the duration of the admission
Heparin infusion for the duration of the admission
Enoxaparin until cessation of the intravenous infusion
Warfarin until cessation of the intravenous infusion
attributes.explanation: This patient has multiple risk factors for VTE, including malignancy, chemotherapy and prolonged immobilisation. Hence, it would be appropriate for him to be placed on some form of pharmacological prophylaxis during his admission. Low-molecular-weight heparin, such as enoxaparin, is the first-line for thromboprophylaxis. It has been shown to be superior to warfarin for reducing VTE risk in patients with cancer and does not significantly increase risk of major bleeding. Mechanical prophylaxis alone, such as compression stockings, would offer insufficient prophylaxis for this patient. In practice outpatients receiving chemotherapy infusions do not receive prophylactic enoxaparin as they are generally mobile.
learningPoints: Enoxaparin is first-line thromboprophylaxis in patients with cancer
references: Abdol Razak NB, Jones G, Bhandari M, Berndt MC, Metharom P. Cancer-Associated Thrombosis: An Overview of Mechanisms, Risk Factors, and Treatment. Cancers (Basel). 2018;10(10):380. doi:10.3390/cancers10100380

Ay C, Kamphuisen PW, Agnelli G. Antithrombotic therapy for prophylaxis and treatment of venous thromboembolism in patients with cancer: review of the literature on current practice and emerging options ESMO Open 2017;2:e000188. doi: 10.1136/esmoopen-2017-000188

Lloyd NS, Douketis JD, Moinuddin I, Lim W, Crowther MA. Anticoagulant prophylaxis to prevent asymptomatic deep vein thrombosis in hospitalized medical patients. Journal of Thrombosis and Haemostasis. 2008;6(3):405–414.
